text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the gastropag limit, exposing the right crus. The esophagus is dissected out, and attachments of the stomach to the left crus are separated.

The surgeon performs mediastinal dissection of the esophagus using a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection techniques. The vagus nerve is carefully dissected out, encircled, and mobilized away from the esophagus to prepare for the myotomy.

The surgeon begins the myotomy using electrocautery to separate the longitudinal muscles of the esophagus, revealing some circular muscle layers. These layers are cauterized to expose the submucosa. Robotic graspers hold both sides of the myotomy, facilitating the delivery of the esophagus into the abdomen. A hook is used to complete the myotomy, with care taken to avoid thermal injury to the submucosa by occasionally cooling the hook on adjacent tissues.

The surgeon uses retraction arms to pull the esophagus down into the abdomen, folding the muscle layers outward like an open book. The myotomy is extended up to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins and down onto the gastroesophageal junction, continuing approximately two to three centimeters onto the stomach. The vagus nerve is visible traversing the myotomy site.

The camera provides a view of the completed myotomy extending from the mid esophagus down onto the stomach, spanning over six centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction and two centimeters below it. The short gastric vessels are divided to facilitate mobilization of the stomach fundus for the upcoming Dor fundoplication.

The surgeon begins the repair by suturing the left crus of the diaphragm to the left side of the myotomy muscle using a 2-0 Ethibond suture. The angle of His is also tacked to the diaphragmatic crus. The fundus of the stomach is then positioned over the esophagus, covering the myotomy. A stitch is placed into the stomach, connecting to the right side of the myotomy muscle and then to the right crus.

The surgeon places another stitch from the fundus of the stomach to the right side of the myotomy, followed by suturing to the right cruce. The stomach is then tacked to the anterior defect, effectively closing the defect and the hiatus.
This patient did well postoperatively. Post-op esophagram demonstrated no leak. The patient was sent home on a full liquid diet on postoperative day number one.
